name: Slovenia
tagline: From the Slavic principality of Carantania to a European republic on the sunny side of the Alps.
summary: Slovenia's history is the long story of a small Alpine and Adriatic people who preserved a distinct language and identity through more than a thousand years of foreign rule. Slavic ancestors settled the eastern Alps in the sixth and seventh centuries and organised the early principality of Carantania, remembered for its ritual enthronement of dukes in the Slovene tongue. From the late Middle Ages the Slovene lands were gathered into the Habsburg duchies of Carniola, Styria and Carinthia, and remained under Austrian rule for some six centuries. The Reformation gave the Slovenes their first printed books, when Primoz Trubar published a catechism and a primer in 1550 and founded a literary language. Napoleon's short-lived Illyrian Provinces, with Ljubljana as their capital, and a nineteenth-century cultural awakening led by the poet France Preseren prepared the way for modern nationhood. After the fall of Austria-Hungary in 1918 the Slovenes joined the Yugoslav state, becoming after 1945 the most prosperous republic of socialist Yugoslavia, before declaring independence on 25 June 1991 and winning it in the brief Ten-Day War. Slovenia joined NATO and the European Union in 2004 and adopted the euro in 2007.

founding.label: The Declaration of Independence of the Republic of Slovenia
founding.detail: Modern Slovenia dates its statehood to 25 June 1991, when, following a plebiscite held on 23 December 1990 in which 88.5 per cent of all eligible voters chose independence, the Slovenian parliament adopted the Basic Constitutional Charter on the Sovereignty and Independence of the Republic of Slovenia together with a formal Declaration of Independence. The move made Slovenia, with Croatia, the first of Yugoslavia's republics to break away. It provoked the intervention of the Yugoslav People's Army and the Ten-Day War of 27 June to 7 July 1991, which the Slovene Territorial Defence won; the Brioni Declaration of 7 July halted the fighting and led to the withdrawal of federal forces. The deeper roots of Slovene identity reach back to the early Slavic principality of Carantania and to the founding of the literary language by the Protestant reformer Primoz Trubar in 1550.

quickFacts.label: Earliest Slavic polity
quickFacts.value: Carantania, a Slavic principality of the 7th–8th centuries in the eastern Alps

quickFacts.label: Birth of the literary language
quickFacts.value: Primoz Trubar printed the first books in Slovene in 1550 (a catechism and an alphabet primer)

quickFacts.label: Napoleonic capital
quickFacts.value: Ljubljana was capital of the French Illyrian Provinces, 1809–1813

quickFacts.label: Independence declared
quickFacts.value: 25 June 1991, won in the Ten-Day War; the first Yugoslav republic to leave

quickFacts.label: Border arbitration with Croatia
quickFacts.value: Permanent Court of Arbitration award on the Piran Bay boundary, 29 June 2017

quickFacts.label: Euro-Atlantic integration
quickFacts.value: Joined NATO (29 March) and the EU (1 May) in 2004; adopted the euro on 1 January 2007

eras.title: Carantania and the Slavic Settlement of the Eastern Alps
eras.standfirst: Slavic peoples settled the eastern Alps and organised Carantania, an early principality whose dukes were enthroned in the Slovene tongue before Frankish power absorbed the region.
body: The lands of present-day Slovenia lie where the Alps meet the Pannonian plain and the head of the Adriatic, a crossroads settled in turn by Illyrians, Celts who founded the kingdom of Noricum, and Romans, whose colony of Emona stood on the site of modern Ljubljana. As Roman order collapsed, Slavic peoples migrated into the eastern Alpine valleys during the sixth and seventh centuries, becoming the ancestors of the Slovenes and laying down the linguistic and territorial foundation of all later Slovene history.

Out of this settlement emerged Carantania (Karantanija), a Slavic principality whose heartland lay to the north of today's Slovenia, around Klagenfurt in present-day Austrian Carinthia. Pressed by the Avars, the Carantanians allied with neighbouring Bavaria in the eighth century and were drawn into the orbit of the Frankish realm, which brought them under Christian and Frankish overlordship. Carantania is remembered above all for the ancient ritual installation of its dukes, performed in the Slovene language at the Prince's Stone near Krnski grad, a ceremony so distinctive that it endured in altered form into the late Middle Ages.

By the close of the first millennium the eastern Alpine Slavs had been incorporated into the Frankish and then the Holy Roman Empire, and the principality of Carniola is first attested in the late tenth century as a frontier march to the south. Christianisation and the arrival of German and Frankish lords reshaped the region's institutions, yet the Slavic population persisted, and the memory of Carantania and its enthronement rite would later be invoked as evidence of an ancient Slovene political tradition.

events.title: Slavic settlement of the eastern Alps
events.summary: Slavic peoples migrated into the eastern Alpine valleys after the fall of Roman authority, becoming the ancestors of the Slovenes and establishing the linguistic foundation of the later nation.

events.title: Founding of Carantania
events.summary: The Slavic principality of Carantania took shape in the eastern Alps, centred north of present-day Slovenia, and is remembered for the enthronement of its dukes in the Slovene language at the Prince's Stone.

events.title: Carantania under Frankish overlordship
events.summary: Allied with Bavaria against the Avars, Carantania was drawn into the Frankish realm and gradually Christianised, ending its independence and binding the region to the Holy Roman Empire.

events.title: Carniola first attested
events.summary: The march of Carniola, in the central part of present-day Slovenia, appears in the records as a frontier zone of the empire, the nucleus of the duchy that would later define the Slovene lands.

figures.name: Carantanian dukes
figures.role: Rulers of the early Slavic principality
figures.blurb: The dukes of Carantania were installed in a ritual conducted in the Slovene tongue at the Prince's Stone, a ceremony that survived into the late Middle Ages and became a touchstone of Slovene political memory.

figures.name: Samo
figures.blurb: A figure associated in tradition with the early organisation of the Alpine and Pannonian Slavs, invoked in Slovenia's national narrative as a precursor of Carantanian self-rule before Frankish dominance.

pullquote.text: The dukes of Carantania were enthroned in the Slovene language at the Prince's Stone, a ritual unique in medieval Europe.

eras.title: The Habsburg Duchies and the Birth of Written Slovene
eras.standfirst: Gathered into the Habsburg duchies of Carniola, Styria and Carinthia, the Slovene lands endured six centuries of Austrian rule, in which the Reformation gave them their first books and a literary language.
body: Through the high Middle Ages the Slovene-speaking lands were organised into the duchies of Carniola, Styria and Carinthia, ruled by a succession of German and Frankish noble houses. In 1335 Carinthia and Carniola passed into the hands of the Habsburgs, who already held Styria, binding almost all the Slovene lands to the dynasty that would govern them for some six hundred years. Carniola was raised to a duchy in 1364, and its capital, Ljubljana, known to its German rulers as Laibach, grew into the chief city of the Slovene lands. The powerful Counts of Celje rose to European prominence before their line died out in 1456, after which their estates too fell to the Habsburgs.

These centuries brought Ottoman raids, heavy feudal burdens and recurrent peasant uprisings, but also a decisive cultural turning point in the Protestant Reformation. In 1550 the Protestant preacher Primoz Trubar published a catechism and an alphabet primer, the Catechismus and Abecedarium, the first printed books in the Slovene language. Driven by the Reformation's conviction that people should read scripture in their own tongue, Trubar created a literary Slovene where none had existed in print; his work was continued by Jurij Dalmatin, who translated the whole Bible into Slovene in 1584, and Adam Bohoric, who produced the first Slovene grammar in the same year.

The Catholic Counter-Reformation that followed restored the old faith and suppressed Protestant worship, yet it could not erase the body of Slovene writing that had been created. The language survived in church and at home, and the printed word forged in the sixteenth century became the seed of a later national consciousness. Beneath the long surface of Habsburg rule, a distinct Slovene cultural identity had taken root that no dynasty could dissolve.

events.title: Habsburgs acquire Carniola and Carinthia
events.summary: Carinthia and Carniola passed to the Habsburg dynasty, which already held Styria, bringing nearly all the Slovene lands under the rule that would last about six centuries.

events.title: Carniola raised to a duchy
events.summary: Carniola was elevated to the status of a duchy, consolidating the core territorial unit of the Slovene lands, with Ljubljana as its principal city.

events.title: First books printed in Slovene
events.summary: Primoz Trubar published the Catechismus and Abecedarium, the first printed books in the Slovene language, founding the literary language as a product of the Protestant Reformation.

events.title: Dalmatin's Bible and Bohoric's grammar
events.summary: Jurij Dalmatin published a complete Slovene translation of the Bible and Adam Bohoric the first Slovene grammar, proving the new written language fit for the most demanding texts.

events.title: Mercury found at Idrija
events.summary: Mercury was first discovered at Idrija, which grew into one of the world's two largest mercury mines; the site is today recognised by UNESCO as part of the Heritage of Mercury.

figures.name: Primoz Trubar
figures.role: Protestant reformer and founder of literary Slovene
figures.blurb: A Protestant preacher who, in exile, wrote and printed the first books in Slovene in 1550. By rendering the catechism and the alphabet into the vernacular he created a literary language and is honoured as the father of Slovene letters.

figures.name: Jurij Dalmatin
figures.blurb: A student of the Slovene Reformation who completed a full translation of the Bible into Slovene, published in 1584, demonstrating that the young written language could carry the entire scriptural canon.

pullquote.text: In 1550 Primoz Trubar printed the first two books in Slovene, founding a literary language that the Counter-Reformation could suppress but never erase.

eras.title: The Illyrian Provinces and the National Awakening
eras.standfirst: Napoleon's Illyrian Provinces made Ljubljana a capital and stirred Slovene self-awareness; a nineteenth-century cultural awakening, led by the poet France Preseren, turned a literary identity into a political programme.
body: The Napoleonic wars briefly broke the rhythm of Austrian rule. Between 1809 and 1813 Napoleon detached the southern Slovene lands and neighbouring territories from Austria and organised them as the Illyrian Provinces, an autonomous unit of the French Empire with Ljubljana as its capital. French administration brought modernising reforms and, crucially for the Slovenes, gave their language a place in schooling and public life. Though Austria recovered the provinces after Napoleon's fall, confirmed by the Congress of Vienna in 1815, the episode planted the idea that the Slovenes were a people deserving of their own institutions.

The decades that followed saw a flowering of Slovene culture that historians call the national awakening. Its towering figure was the Romantic poet France Preseren, whose verse raised the vernacular to the level of high literature and gave the nation an enduring voice; his 1844 poem Zdravljica, a toast to the freedom and friendship of all peoples, would later furnish the words of the Slovene national anthem. Writers, scholars and clergy built a Slovene press, learned societies and a reading public, converting cultural pride into political ambition.

That ambition crystallised in the revolutionary year of 1848. During the Spring of Nations Slovene activists formulated the programme of a United Slovenia, demanding that all the Slovene-inhabited districts of the Habsburg monarchy be joined into a single autonomous unit, with the Slovene language recognised in administration and education. The programme was not achieved, but it marked the passage from cultural revival to modern national politics and remained the guiding aspiration of the Slovene movement down to the First World War.

events.title: The Illyrian Provinces
events.summary: Napoleon organised the southern Slovene lands as the Illyrian Provinces of the French Empire, with Ljubljana as their capital, introducing reforms and giving the Slovene language a place in public life.

events.title: Austria regains the Slovene lands
events.summary: After Napoleon's defeat the Congress of Vienna restored Habsburg rule over the Illyrian territories, returning the Slovene lands to the Austrian Empire.

events.title: Preseren writes Zdravljica
events.summary: The poet France Preseren composed Zdravljica, a toast to liberty and the brotherhood of nations whose seventh stanza became the lyric of the Slovene national anthem after independence.

events.title: The United Slovenia programme
events.summary: During the Spring of Nations, Slovene activists demanded a single autonomous unit joining all Slovene districts of the monarchy with the Slovene language in public use, marking the birth of modern Slovene politics.

figures.name: France Preseren
figures.blurb: Slovenia's foremost contributor to European Romanticism, Preseren elevated the Slovene language to the highest literary art. His poem Zdravljica, calling for the freedom and equality of nations, became the source of the national anthem.

figures.name: Primoz Trubar
figures.blurb: The Reformation-era writer whose first Slovene books made possible the later literary and national revival; his legacy was repeatedly invoked by the awakeners of the nineteenth century as proof of the language's antiquity and dignity.

pullquote.text: The United Slovenia programme of 1848 marked the transition from cultural initiatives to the modern political articulation of the Slovene nation.
pullquote.attribution: Government of Slovenia, on the national awakening

eras.title: Into Yugoslavia: War, Partition and Contested Borders
eras.standfirst: The fall of Austria-Hungary drew the Slovenes into a Yugoslav state, but the new frontiers were bitterly contested: Carinthia voted to stay with Austria, Italy seized the western marches, and the Second World War tore the land into three occupation zones.
body: When Austria-Hungary collapsed at the end of the First World War, the Slovenes joined the other South Slavs of the former empire in the short-lived State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs, proclaimed in October 1918. On 1 December 1918 this entity merged with the kingdom of Serbia to form the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes, renamed the Kingdom of Yugoslavia in 1929. For the first time the bulk of the Slovenes lived in a Slavic state of their own, though one in which power lay heavily with Belgrade.

The peace settlements left the Slovene nation divided by hotly disputed borders. To the north, the future of southern Carinthia was decided by plebiscite on 10 October 1920: in the contested basin, where many voters were Slovene-speaking, a majority of about 59 per cent chose to remain with the Republic of Austria, leaving a Slovene minority on the Austrian side of the frontier. To the west, under the Treaty of Rapallo of 1920 Italy acquired the Slovene Littoral, the Karst and parts of Inner Carniola, placing a large Slovene population under Italian rule and, after 1922, under Fascist policies of forced assimilation.

The Second World War brought catastrophe. In April 1941 the Axis powers invaded and dismembered Yugoslavia, partitioning the Slovene lands among Nazi Germany, which annexed the north, Fascist Italy, which took the centre and Ljubljana, and Hungary, which seized the Prekmurje region in the east. A Slovene Liberation Front was founded in Ljubljana on 26 April 1941 and joined Tito's Partisan movement; after years of occupation, resistance and bitter internal conflict, Slovene territory was liberated and the war ended in May 1945.

events.title: Birth of the Yugoslav kingdom
events.summary: After the brief State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs, the Slovene lands joined the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes, proclaimed on 1 December 1918 and renamed the Kingdom of Yugoslavia in 1929.

events.title: The Carinthian Plebiscite
events.summary: In the disputed southern Carinthian basin, a majority of about 59 per cent voted to remain with Austria rather than join Yugoslavia, leaving a Slovene minority on the Austrian side of the border.

events.title: Treaty of Rapallo and the Italian border
events.summary: Under the Treaty of Rapallo, Italy gained the Slovene Littoral, the Karst and parts of Inner Carniola, subjecting a large Slovene population to Italian and later Fascist rule.

events.title: Axis partition of Slovenia
events.summary: Following the invasion of Yugoslavia, the Axis powers carved up the Slovene lands among Germany, Italy and Hungary, while a Liberation Front founded in Ljubljana launched armed resistance.

figures.name: Anton Korosec
figures.blurb: A priest and statesman who led the Slovene People's Party and presided over the National Council that proclaimed the State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs in 1918, the only Slovene to head a Yugoslav government.

figures.name: Edvard Kardelj
figures.blurb: A Slovene communist who helped found the Liberation Front in 1941 and became one of the leading theorists of postwar Yugoslavia, shaping its system of self-management and federal structure.

pullquote.text: In the Carinthian plebiscite of 10 October 1920 the contested basin voted, by about 59 per cent, to remain part of Austria.

eras.title: Socialist Yugoslavia and the Road to Independence
eras.standfirst: As the most prosperous republic of socialist Yugoslavia, Slovenia settled its western border with Italy, then broke away in 1991 in the first secession from the Yugoslav federation, won in the Ten-Day War.
body: After 1945 Slovenia became one of the six federal republics of the Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia under Josip Broz Tito. Its highly developed industry, education and proximity to Western Europe made it consistently the wealthiest and most productive of the Yugoslav republics, even as it chafed at the redistribution of its earnings to poorer regions. The post-war years also finally settled the western frontier: the contested Free Territory of Trieste was provisionally divided by the London Memorandum of 1954, which placed Zone A under Italian and Zone B under Yugoslav administration, and the arrangement was made permanent by the Treaty of Osimo, signed by Italy and Yugoslavia on 10 November 1975.

Tito's death in 1980 loosened the bonds of the federation, and through the 1980s Slovenia became the centre of demands for democratisation and greater autonomy. Civil society, independent media and an emerging political opposition pressed against the one-party system, while Slovene leaders resisted attempts to recentralise Yugoslavia under Serbian dominance. In a plebiscite held on 23 December 1990, with a turnout of 93.2 per cent, some 88.5 per cent of all eligible voters chose an independent Slovenia.

On 25 June 1991 the Slovenian parliament adopted the Basic Constitutional Charter on Sovereignty and Independence and a Declaration of Independence, making Slovenia, with Croatia, the first republic to leave Yugoslavia. The federal army moved to prevent the secession, triggering the Ten-Day War of 27 June to 7 July 1991, in which the lightly armed Slovene Territorial Defence fought the Yugoslav People's Army to a standstill. The European Community brokered the Brioni Declaration of 7 July 1991, which suspended the independence measures for three months and arranged the withdrawal of federal forces; Slovenia's independence soon became irreversible.

events.title: Slovenia within socialist Yugoslavia
events.summary: Slovenia became one of the six republics of Tito's Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia, developing into its most prosperous and industrialised member.

events.title: London Memorandum on Trieste
events.summary: The United States, United Kingdom, Italy and Yugoslavia agreed to divide the Free Territory of Trieste, placing Zone A under Italian and Zone B under Yugoslav administration.

events.title: Treaty of Osimo
events.summary: Italy and Yugoslavia signed the Treaty of Osimo, making the partition of the former Free Territory of Trieste definitive and settling the long-disputed western border.

events.title: Plebiscite on independence
events.summary: In a plebiscite with 93.2 per cent turnout, some 88.5 per cent of all eligible Slovene voters chose independence from Yugoslavia, providing the mandate for statehood.

events.title: Independence and the Ten-Day War
events.summary: Slovenia declared independence on 25 June 1991; the Yugoslav army's intervention sparked the Ten-Day War, which the Slovenes won, leading to the Brioni Declaration of 7 July and the withdrawal of federal forces.

figures.name: Milan Kucan
figures.blurb: The last head of the Slovene communist party and the country's first president, Kucan steered Slovenia from the loosening of Yugoslavia through the 1990 plebiscite and the 1991 declaration of independence.

figures.name: Josip Broz Tito
figures.blurb: The Partisan commander and lifelong president of Yugoslavia under whom Slovenia became the federation's most prosperous republic; his death in 1980 began the unravelling that ended in Slovene independence.

pullquote.text: On 25 June 1991 Slovenia declared its independence, becoming, with Croatia, the first republic to leave Yugoslavia.
pullquote.attribution: On the birth of the Slovene state

eras.title: A European Republic and the Border Arbitration
eras.standfirst: Independent Slovenia anchored itself in NATO and the European Union, adopted the euro, and pursued a long-running maritime border dispute with Croatia to international arbitration.
body: Free of Yugoslavia, Slovenia built a parliamentary democracy and a market economy and turned decisively toward Western institutions. It joined the Council of Europe and the United Nations early in the decade, and its stable politics and relative prosperity smoothed a rapid integration into the Euro-Atlantic order. On 29 March 2004 Slovenia became a member of NATO, and on 1 May 2004 it joined the European Union in the bloc's largest single enlargement, the only former Yugoslav republic in that wave. On 1 January 2007 it became the first of the new member states to adopt the euro.

One legacy of the breakup of Yugoslavia, however, proved durable: the unresolved land and maritime border with neighbouring Croatia, centred on the Gulf of Piran (Piran Bay) at the head of the Adriatic, where the two republics' coasts meet and Slovenia's access to the open sea was at stake. After years of dispute the two countries signed an Arbitration Agreement on 4 November 2009 and submitted the question to the Permanent Court of Arbitration in The Hague. On 29 June 2017 the tribunal rendered its Final Award, determining the entire land boundary, drawing the maritime boundary through Piran Bay, and establishing a junction giving Slovenia access to the high seas through Croatian waters.

The award did not end the quarrel. Slovenia welcomed the decision as binding and historic, but Croatia, which had earlier withdrawn from the proceedings, rejected the ruling and declined to implement it; Slovenia later brought the matter before the European institutions. Within Europe, however, Slovenia had established itself as a small, settled and prosperous state, its identity rooted in the Slovene language first put into print nearly five centuries earlier and in the hard-won independence of 1991. Its natural and industrial heritage is recognised internationally, from the karst of the Skocjan Caves to the great mercury mine of Idrija, both inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list.

events.title: Slovenia joins NATO
events.summary: After a 2003 referendum approved by some two-thirds of voters, Slovenia became a member of NATO on 29 March 2004, anchoring its security in the Western alliance.

events.title: Accession to the European Union
events.summary: Slovenia joined the European Union on 1 May 2004 in the bloc's largest enlargement, the only former Yugoslav republic to accede in that wave.

events.title: Adoption of the euro
events.summary: Slovenia became the first of the 2004 entrants to adopt the euro, replacing the tolar on 1 January 2007 and completing its integration into the core of the European Union.

events.title: Arbitration Agreement with Croatia
events.summary: Slovenia and Croatia signed an Arbitration Agreement submitting their land and maritime border dispute, including Piran Bay, to the Permanent Court of Arbitration in The Hague.

events.title: PCA award on the Piran Bay border
events.summary: The Permanent Court of Arbitration rendered its Final Award, fixing the land and maritime boundary through Piran Bay and granting Slovenia a junction to the high seas; Slovenia accepted the award, Croatia rejected it.

figures.name: Janez Drnovsek
figures.blurb: The dominant statesman of Slovenia's first independent decade, Drnovsek led the governments that stabilised the economy and negotiated entry into NATO and the European Union, and later served as president.

figures.name: Borut Pahor
figures.blurb: Prime minister when the 2009 Arbitration Agreement with Croatia was signed and later president, he was a central figure in Slovenia's pursuit of a settled border and its place in the European Union.

pullquote.text: On 29 June 2017 the Permanent Court of Arbitration fixed the boundary through Piran Bay and a junction to the high seas; Slovenia accepted it, Croatia rejected it.

figures.name: Primoz Trubar
figures.role: Founder of the Slovene literary language
figures.blurb: The Protestant reformer who printed the first books in Slovene in 1550, creating a written language and earning recognition as the father of Slovene letters.

figures.name: France Preseren
figures.blurb: Slovenia's greatest Romantic poet, whose work raised the vernacular to high art and whose poem Zdravljica became the words of the national anthem.

figures.name: Milan Kucan
figures.blurb: The leader who guided Slovenia from the 1990 independence plebiscite through the 1991 declaration and the Ten-Day War to internationally recognised statehood.

figures.name: Josip Broz Tito
figures.blurb: The Yugoslav leader under whom Slovenia became the federation's most prosperous republic; his death in 1980 set in motion the events that led to Slovene independence.
